questions.prompt: Complete: "This is the restaurant ___ we had dinner last week."
questions.explanation: Use where for a place (= in which): the restaurant where we had dinner. "which" would need the place as an object (the restaurant which we liked), "who" is for people, and "whose" shows possession.

questions.prompt: Complete: "He's the writer ___ books sell millions of copies."
questions.explanation: whose shows possession — it replaces his here (his books): the writer whose books sell. "who" needs a verb after it (the writer who writes), "which" is for things, and "where" is for places.

questions.prompt: Complete: "That's the stadium ___ the final was played."
questions.explanation: Use where for a place (= in which): the stadium where the final was played. "who" is for people, "whose" shows possession, and bare "which" would need the place as an object (the stadium which they built).

questions.prompt: Complete: "That's the neighbour ___ dog barks all night."
questions.explanation: whose shows possession (his/her dog): the neighbour whose dog barks. "who's" means "who is" (the neighbour who's angry), "who" needs a verb directly after it, and "which" is for things, not a person.

questions.prompt: Combine into one sentence: "I visited a museum. The museum had no entrance fee."

options.text: I visited a museum where had no entrance fee.
I visited a museum which had no entrance fee.
I visited a museum who had no entrance fee.
I visited a museum whose had no entrance fee.

questions.explanation: The museum is a thing and the subject of "had", so use which: a museum which had no entrance fee. "where" can't be the subject of "had" (a), "who" is for people (c), and "whose" needs a noun after it (d).

options.text: The hotel where we stayed was cheap.
The friend whose car we borrowed lives in Tunis.
The pen where I write with is broken.
The street where I grew up has changed a lot.

questions.explanation: In (c), "the pen where I write with" is wrong: a pen is a thing, so use which or that (the pen which I write with). "where" is only for places. (a), (b) and (d) use where and whose correctly.
